payload.contentString:
{patientName} is a 12-year-old boy from the Czech Republic who comes to clinic with episodes concerning for recurrent seizures, consistent with epilepsy as a brain disorder causing recurring seizures [MEDLINEPLUS:G40.9]. His family reports that some events occur on awakening from sleep, when he suddenly becomes panicked and has violent vomiting without fever or other signs of acute illness [MEDDIALOG:G40.9:1]. Between episodes he is generally well appearing, attends school regularly, and spends his free time playing football with friends and video games with his older cousin. He lives with his parents, who are very attentive and worried about the unpredictability of these episodes.

During some spells, {patientName} seems briefly confused and behaves strangely, which fits the description that people with epilepsy may have strange sensations or emotions or behave strangely during seizures [MEDLINEPLUS:G40.9]. His parents have not consistently witnessed full-body convulsions, but they are frightened by the abrupt nocturnal or early-morning onset and the repeated vomiting [MEDDIALOG:G40.9:1]. He has no fever during these episodes [MEDDIALOG:G40.9:1].

At school, {patientName} is described as an active student who enjoys art and science and usually has good energy. He is embarrassed by the possibility of having an event in front of classmates, so he has recently become more cautious about sleepovers and late-night activities. His parents have started watching his sleep schedule more closely because sleep deprivation can precipitate some epileptic syndromes with events on awakening [MEDDIALOG:G40.9:1].

He presents today looking alert and cooperative, with age-appropriate growth and normal conversation. His family hopes to clarify whether these recurrent spells represent epilepsy, since epilepsy may have no clearly identifiable cause in many cases [MEDLINEPLUS:G40.9]. They are eager for testing and treatment because early diagnosis and treatment are important [MEDLINEPLUS:G40.9].

{patientName} has had several recurrent nighttime or early-morning episodes over recent days, with violent vomiting on awakening, panic, and no fever or sick symptoms between events [MEDDIALOG:G40.9:1]. The family sought evaluation because they were concerned these repeated spells could represent epilepsy [MEDDIALOG:G40.9:1]. There is no documented prior brain injury or known structural neurologic disorder, and the cause of epilepsy is often unknown [MEDLINEPLUS:G40.9].

He has not had a previous epilepsy surgery or implanted device. He has not required prior intensive care admission based on the available history. Because sleep deprivation can precipitate some epileptic syndromes with events on awakening, the family has recently tried to maintain a more regular bedtime schedule for {patientName} [MEDDIALOG:G40.9:1]. He does not use alcohol, and there is no smoking exposure reported in his personal habits.

Given the chronic nature of epilepsy and the risk of seizure recurrence if treatment is reduced or stopped, long-term antiseizure therapy may be needed once the diagnosis is confirmed and treatment is started [MEDDIALOG:G40.9:4].
